category_summary:
A critical **PyPI supply chain attack** on **litellm** dominated the day, with **Andrej Karpathy**'s [viral exposé](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-f4f8894e9c8b) (26.5M views) detailing how a simple `pip install` could exfiltrate SSH keys and cloud credentials. **Jim Fan** [extended the alarm](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-449415af2b95), warning that agentic coding tools create unprecedented identity-theft attack surfaces.

- **Sam Altman** [announced the **OpenAI Foundation**'s](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-45c66ddb249e) $1B first-year spending plan focused on scientific discovery, bio threats, and economic disruption — with **Bret Taylor** confirming board-level backing
- **Boris Cherny** [revealed **Anthropic**'s small Labs team](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-8e2526109879) shipped MCP, Claude Desktop, and Claude Code, then announced the [removal of permission prompts](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-aae4fa235251) in **Claude Code** — one of the most celebrated UX changes in AI tooling this cycle
- **Anthropic** [published engineering details](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-d013489ab7f4) on a multi-agent harness pushing **Claude** further in frontend design and autonomous software engineering, alongside new [Economic Index research](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-67a18ba3ae8b) showing experienced users actually hand over *less* autonomy
- **Allen AI** [released **MolmoWeb**](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-3058194dbcef), an open-source browser agent achieving new SOTA across web-agent benchmarks, while **OpenAI** quietly [killed **Sora**](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-16165fb9b09d) in what **swyx** called the first casualty of their 'Side Quests' crackdown
- The **Figma MCP** [integration with **Claude Code**](/?date=2026-03-25&category=social#item-100904bebb69) signaled a maturing design-to-code pipeline that drew strong developer enthusiasm

items.title:
Software horror: litellm PyPI supply chain attack. 

Simple `pip install litellm` was enough to exfi...
items.content:
Software horror: litellm PyPI supply chain attack. 

Simple `pip install litellm` was enough to exfiltrate SSH keys, AWS/GCP/Azure creds, Kubernetes configs, git credentials, env vars (all your API keys), shell history, crypto wallets, SSL private keys, CI/CD secrets, database passwords.

LiteLLM itself has 97 million downloads per month which is already terrible, but much worse, the contagion spreads to any project that depends on litellm. For example, if you did `pip install dspy` (which depended on litellm>=1.64.0), you'd also be pwnd. Same for any other large project that depended on litellm.

Afaict the poisoned version was up for only less than ~1 hour. The attack had a bug which led to its discovery - Callum McMahon was using an MCP plugin inside Cursor that pulled in litellm as a transitive dependency. When litellm 1.82.8 installed, their machine ran out of RAM and crashed. So if the attacker didn't vibe code this attack it could have been undetected for many days or weeks.

Supply chain attacks like this are basically the scariest thing imaginable in modern software. Every time you install any depedency you could be pulling in a poisoned package anywhere deep inside its entire depedency tree. This is especially risky with large projects that might have lots and lots of dependencies. The credentials that do get stolen in each attack can then be used to take over more accounts and compromise more packages.

Classical software engineering would have you believe that dependencies are good (we're building pyramids from bricks), but imo this has to be re-evaluated, and it's why I've been so growingly averse to them, preferring to use LLMs to "yoink" functionality when it's simple enough and possible.

items.title: AI will help discover new science, such as cures for diseases, which is perhaps the most important w...
items.author: @sama
items.summary: Sam Altman announces the OpenAI Foundation's initial focus areas: AI-driven scientific discovery, novel bio threats, economic disruption, and emergent societal effects. The Foundation will spend at least $1B in the next year. Key hires include Wojciech Zaremba as Head of AI Resilience, plus heads for Life Sciences, Civil Society, CFO and Director of Operations.
items.reasoning: Major organizational announcement from OpenAI CEO with enormous engagement (663K views, 5.4K likes). $1B Foundation spending commitment and senior leadership transitions are highly consequential for the AI ecosystem.
items.summary_html: Sam Altman announces the OpenAI Foundation's initial focus areas: AI-driven scientific discovery, novel bio threats, economic disruption, and emergent societal effects. The Foundation will spend at least $1B in the next year. Key hires include Wojciech Zaremba as Head of AI Resilience, plus heads for Life Sciences, Civil Society, CFO and Director of Operations.
items.content_html:
AI will help discover new science, such as cures for diseases, which is perhaps the most important way to increase quality of life long-term.

AI will also present new threats to society that we have to address. No company can sufficiently mitigate these on their own; we will need a society-wide response to things like novel bio threats, a massive and fast change to the economy, extremely capable models causing complex emergent effects across society, and more.

These are the areas the OpenAI Foundation will initially focus on, and in my opinion are some of the most important ones for us to get right. The Foundation will spend at least $1 billion over the next year.

@woj_zaremba, co-founder of OpenAI, will transition to Head of AI Resilience. I believe that shifting how the world thinks about safety to include a Resilience-style approach is critical, and I am extremely grateful to Wojciech for taking on this role.

Wojciech has been my cofounder for the last decade; anyone who knows him will understand what I mean when I say he is one of a kind. He has a lot of ideas about how we build a new kind of AI safety.

@JacobTref is joining as Head of Life Sciences and Curing Diseases.

@annaadeola, our VP of Global Impact, will transition to Head of AI for Civil Society and Philanthropy.

@robert_kaiden is joining as Chief Financial Officer.

@jeffarnold is joining as Director of Operations.

items.title:
Little known fact, the Anthropic Labs team (the team I joined Anthropic to be on) shipped:

- MCP
- ...
items.content:
Little known fact, the Anthropic Labs team (the team I joined Anthropic to be on) shipped:

- MCP
- Skills
- Claude Desktop app
- Claude Code

It was just a few of us, shipping fast, trying to keep pace with what the model was capable of.

Those early Desktop computer use prototypes, back in the Sonnet 3.6 days, felt clunky and slow. But it was easy to squint and imagine all the ways people might use it once it got really good.

Fast forward to today. I am so excited to release full computer use in Cowork and Dispatch. Really excited to see what you do with it!
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: Building on yesterday's [Social](/?date=2026-03-24&category=social#item-53256ddda621) buzz, Boris Cherny reveals the small Anthropic Labs team shipped MCP, Skills, Claude Desktop, and Claude Code. Now announces full computer use in Cowork and Dispatch features.
items.reasoning: Massive engagement (8590 likes, 878K views). Major revelation about Anthropic's product team structure. Announces full computer use in new Cowork and Dispatch features. First-hand insider perspective on Claude's evolution from early computer use prototypes.

continuation.original_title: Claude Code can control your computer now!

items.summary_html: Building on yesterday's Social buzz, Boris Cherny reveals the small Anthropic Labs team shipped MCP, Skills, Claude Desktop, and Claude Code. Now announces full computer use in Cowork and Dispatch features.
items.content_html:
Little known fact, the Anthropic Labs team (the team I joined Anthropic to be on) shipped:

  • MCP
  • Skills
  • Claude Desktop app
  • Claude Code

It was just a few of us, shipping fast, trying to keep pace with what the model was capable of.

Those early Desktop computer use prototypes, back in the Sonnet 3.6 days, felt clunky and slow. But it was easy to squint and imagine all the ways people might use it once it got really good.

Fast forward to today. I am so excited to release full computer use in Cowork and Dispatch. Really excited to see what you do with it!

items.title: This is pure nightmare fuel. Identity theft of the past would be nothing compared to what vibe agent...
items.content:
This is pure nightmare fuel. Identity theft of the past would be nothing compared to what vibe agents can do. Sending credentials is too obvious and for rookies. They could easily spread contaminations across ~/.claude, **/skills/*, or even just a PDF your agent visits periodically in /morning-brief. Your entire filesystem is the new distributed codebase. Every file that could go into context would add to the attack vector. Every text can be a base64 virus.

In the new world of on-demand software, I try to minimize dependencies - people rarely need all the APIs supported in LiteLLM, might as well build a custom router with only what you need on the fly (which I did in one of my late-night claude sessions).

Unfortunately, there is very little middleground between "pressing yes mindlessly for every edit" and "--dangerously-skip-permissions". There will be a full blooming industry for "de-vibing": dampening the slop and putting guardrails/accountability around agentic frameworks. They are the boring old, audited Software 1.0 that watches over the rebellious adolescents of Software 3.0. 

Claws need shells. Probably many layers of nested shells.
items.author: @DrJimFan
items.summary: Jim Fan warns about security dangers of agentic AI coding tools, describing how identity theft via vibe agents could contaminate filesystems, config files, and context windows. Argues there's little middle ground between over-permissioning and blocking everything, predicting a new 'de-vibing' industry for guardrails around agentic frameworks.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful, high-engagement post (80K views, 454 likes) from a prominent NVIDIA researcher directly connecting to the litellm supply chain attack, adding depth on agentic security risks. Original framing of 'de-vibing' as an emerging industry.

New from the Anthropic Economic Index: how people’s use of Claude changes with experience.

Longer-term users are more likely to iterate carefully with Claude, and less likely to hand it full autonomy. They attempt higher-value tasks, and receive more successful responses. https://t.co/yCsrA0bLt9
items.author: @AnthropicAI
items.summary: Anthropic's Economic Index research finds that longer-term Claude users iterate more carefully, hand over less full autonomy, attempt higher-value tasks, and receive more successful responses. Consumer use has also become less concentrated across task types.

Last fall, OpenAI announced its recapitalization, paving the way for the OpenAI Foundation to access significant resources. On behalf of the Board, I’m excited to share an update today on how the Foundation is starting to put that support to work – including our plans to spend at least $1B over the next year and the team we’re putting together.

Our work is just beginning. We’ll have more updates to share in the coming weeks and months. https://t.co/lmNcAa3UUR

items.title: People struggle to differentiate fluid intelligence from knowledge because, given enough preparation...
items.content: People struggle to differentiate fluid intelligence from knowledge because, given enough preparation, memorized templates become a solid substitute for on-the-fly adaptation

Local AI is free, fast & secure!

So today we're introducing hf-mount: attach any storage bucket, model or dataset from @huggingface as a local filesystem.

This is a game changer, as it allows you to attach remote storage that is 100x bigger than your local machine's disk.  This is also perfect for Agentic storage!! 

Let's go!

items.title:
@AvivTamar1 @pulkitology JEPA is a class of architectures, not a method. 
There were lots of JEPAs b...
items.content:
@AvivTamar1 @pulkitology JEPA is a class of architectures, not a method. 
There were lots of JEPAs before I proposed the name as a unifying concept. 

For example, Siamese nets (NIPS 1993) are a special case of JEPA (where the predictor is the identity, and the training loss is contrastive as in SimCLR).

The question is how the architecture is trained, particularly how you prevent collapse. 

Many world models are JEPAs that are not trained as JEPA but using a reconstruction criterion (e.g. Dreamer models and Wayve's Gaia which use VAE or VQVAE).

Predicting an action between two states ("inverse dynamics" as in Poke by Poking) is a weak way to prevent collapse.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Yann LeCun provides a detailed technical explanation of JEPA as a class of architectures, clarifying its relationship to Siamese nets, world models, and various training approaches including collapse prevention.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical explanation from a Turing Award winner (12K views, 230 likes). Clarifies important architectural concepts central to LeCun's research agenda.
items.summary_html: Yann LeCun provides a detailed technical explanation of JEPA as a class of architectures, clarifying its relationship to Siamese nets, world models, and various training approaches including collapse prevention.
items.content_html:
@AvivTamar1 @pulkitology JEPA is a class of architectures, not a method.

There were lots of JEPAs before I proposed the name as a unifying concept.

For example, Siamese nets (NIPS 1993) are a special case of JEPA (where the predictor is the identity, and the training loss is contrastive as in SimCLR).

The question is how the architecture is trained, particularly how you prevent collapse.

Many world models are JEPAs that are not trained as JEPA but using a reconstruction criterion (e.g. Dreamer models and Wayve's Gaia which use VAE or VQVAE).

Predicting an action between two states ("inverse dynamics" as in Poke by Poking) is a weak way to prevent collapse.

items.content: Big lesson from high reliability organizations that AI agent builders need to learn is reliability is the property of systems. Current agentic tools are weaker than the agents: they are bad at agent-agent handoffs, escalation, when to call in humans. All keys to high reliability.

vLLM in 2026: Architectural Chall...
items.content:
Missed our live talk at #GTC2026? Here's what you need to know. 👇

vLLM in 2026: Architectural Challenges and Performance Optimizations, by @woosuk_k 

- Model Runner V2 (MRV2): GPU-native Triton kernels replace CPU PyTorch ops
- Hybrid Memory Allocator: 0–12% memory waste across OSS models
- Encoder Prefill Disaggregation: up to 2.5x P99 throughput for multimodal workloads
- ModularKernel for MoE: mix-and-match GEMM + all-to-all kernels
- Case study: Kimi K2.5 (NVFP4) on GB200

🔗 Slides: https://t.co/PWptHIyMnt

#vLLM #GTC2026 #LLMInference #NVIDIA

items.title:
Yes, these agents are absolutely horrible at writing decent-quality code.

But any time you say that...
items.content:
Yes, these agents are absolutely horrible at writing decent-quality code.

But any time you say that out loud, there's a swarm of AI-apologists who've never built anything that scream at you, "skill issue!"

You might not care about the code, and that's alright, but you can't gaslight anyone who cares about good code.

items.title: You might ask, if competence can be achieved either way (by exhaustive preparation, or by having hig...
items.content:
You might ask, if competence can be achieved either way (by exhaustive preparation, or by having higher intelligence), why would we even care about creating actual intelligence? Isn't collecting dense enough training data good enough to achieve the goal?

Intelligence is a multiplier for knowledge. Given the same information access, the higher-intelligence system will always be more competent, and inversely, intelligence lets you reach a given level of competency with a lot less training, thus much more cheaply
items.author: @fchollet

items.title: When high-fluid intelligence systems start to show up, they will immediately take over the knowledge...
items.content: When high-fluid intelligence systems start to show up, they will immediately take over the knowledge-dependent ones. Because they will be able to scale their knowledge just as well as legacy systems (knowledge gathering is the easy part), while their ability to recombine and apply that knowledge will create a performance gap that preparation alone will never bridge

items.title: I grew up hacking on and building with early open source software - Linux, Apache, GCC. For me, a bi...
items.content:
I grew up hacking on and building with early open source software - Linux, Apache, GCC. For me, a big part of the culture of open source was people making their software their own.

I have been thinking about open source in the age of coding agents. There is one world where many open source projects become less important because we all independently recreate the functionality with coding agents.

Is there another where open source platforms create an ecosystem with their harness and agentic hackability, like OpenClaw has done? If an open source database or content management system shipped with an exceptional agent harness that made easy to extend and improve with agents, would that make individual developers more inclined to start there rather than start from scratch? Likewise, in the spirit of the hacker culture I grew up with, will power users like me start to seek out systems that are designed to be hackable with agents?

I’d love a desktop operating system that was more hackable by casual users like me - not just the gratuitous window dressing of the early Linux desktop movement, but set of composable graphics and OS abstractions designed to be composed, mutated, and rearranged by an agent.

After 25+ years, maybe this really is the year of Linux on the desktop 😏
items.author: @btaylor
items.summary: Bret Taylor reflects on open source in the age of coding agents, wondering whether agentic hackability could make open source platforms more attractive than rebuilding from scratch, and imagining agent-hackable desktop operating systems.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful essay from Sierra CEO and former Salesforce co-CEO (15K views, 130 likes). Original thinking about how coding agents reshape the open source ecosystem.
items.summary_html: Bret Taylor reflects on open source in the age of coding agents, wondering whether agentic hackability could make open source platforms more attractive than rebuilding from scratch, and imagining agent-hackable desktop operating systems.
items.content_html:
I grew up hacking on and building with early open source software - Linux, Apache, GCC. For me, a big part of the culture of open source was people making their software their own.

I have been thinking about open source in the age of coding agents. There is one world where many open source projects become less important because we all independently recreate the functionality with coding agents.

Is there another where open source platforms create an ecosystem with their harness and agentic hackability, like OpenClaw has done? If an open source database or content management system shipped with an exceptional agent harness that made easy to extend and improve with agents, would that make individual developers more inclined to start there rather than start from scratch? Likewise, in the spirit of the hacker culture I grew up with, will power users like me start to seek out systems that are designed to be hackable with agents?

I’d love a desktop operating system that was more hackable by casual users like me - not just the gratuitous window dressing of the early Linux desktop movement, but set of composable graphics and OS abstractions designed to be composed, mutated, and rearranged by an agent.

After 25+ years, maybe this really is the year of Linux on the desktop 😏

items.title:
The AI hardware race just got a very well-funded new player.

Brett Adcock spent 8 months in stealth...
items.content:
The AI hardware race just got a very well-funded new player.

Brett Adcock spent 8 months in stealth and $100M of his own money building Hark, a new AI lab targeting what he calls "the most advanced personal intelligence in the world."

The team: 45+ engineers and designers pulled from Apple, Tesla, Google, Meta, and Amazon. The designer behind iPhone Air is leading hardware design.

NVIDIA is supplying thousands of B200 GPUs for Hark expected online by April.

The startup's first model is expected this summer.

Adcock: "We are crafting a new interface to AGI. Intelligence that lets you offload your mental workload into a system that begins to think like you and sometimes ahead of you."

items.title:
In the past:

1. A startup invests significant resources into a novel software and offers it for a f...
items.content:
In the past:

1. A startup invests significant resources into a novel software and offers it for a fee.

2. If the software becomes relatively popular, an open-source alternative emerges and lags behind for a significant time, giving the startup time to establish a stable client base.

Today:

1. A startup invests significant resources into software and offers it for a fee.

2. Independently of popularity, a dozen early adopters vibe-code a dozen open-source alternatives. No one makes money.

Pure software startups are dead, IMO. Change my mind.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues pure software startups are dead because vibe coding enables rapid open-source alternatives before startups can establish client bases, unlike in the past.

Today:

Pure software startups are dead, IMO. Change my mind.

items.title: I wish I could see some of those millions of companies making lots of money out of vibe-coded soluti...
items.content:
I wish I could see some of those millions of companies making lots of money out of vibe-coded solutions.

Without real names, it looks more like the second dotcom boom to me: everyone has a https://t.co/12PiM6acok (a vibe-coded software project) without any paying customers.

I have been arguing this for a long time: the bottleneck is not the inability of people to code. The bottleneck is the lack of ideas that software startup accelerators haven't brute-forced yet.

The money should be on the intersection of software and real world. And this is where vibe coding breaks its teeth.

items.title:
A repo just turned AI coding agents into real engineers.

Most people fire up Claude Code or Codex a...
items.content:
A repo just turned AI coding agents into real engineers.

Most people fire up Claude Code or Codex and let it go.

The agent guesses what you want. It skips tests and produces spaghetti code. 

A GitHub repo called Superpowers fixes that. 

It forces a strict workflow on your agent:

> Brainstorm with you first
> Builds a detailed implementation plan
> Fresh subagents handle each task
> Two-stage review after every task
> Test-driven development is mandatory

Before any code exists, a failing test must. Code written before tests gets deleted.

The agent works autonomously for hours. No prompting tricks are needed.

Install it and the skills activate automatically. 

Works with Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode.

This is not a prompt template. It's an operating system for AI-assisted development.  

40.9K GitHub stars. Fully open-source.

items.title: @jadenitripp @laf131 To our knowledge we're the only major image model that doesn't totally rewrite ...
items.content: @jadenitripp @laf131 To our knowledge we're the only major image model that doesn't totally rewrite your prompts with a LLM before going into the system! Do you have a preference for how our system should behave if you put something that's too long into it? We find that most of the long prompts have details that have no impact on the image so it might be easier for most people to do our best to losslessly compress the prompt then let them know it happened etc.
items.author: @midjourney
items.summary: Midjourney reveals they're the only major image model that doesn't rewrite prompts with an LLM before processing, and asks users for feedback on handling prompts that exceed token limits, suggesting lossless compression
items.reasoning: Significant product insight from Midjourney - unique technical approach of not using LLM prompt rewriting. Reveals architectural decision and solicits community feedback. Important for understanding image generation ecosystem.
items.summary_html: Midjourney reveals they're the only major image model that doesn't rewrite prompts with an LLM before processing, and asks users for feedback on handling prompts that exceed token limits, suggesting lossless compression
items.content_html: @jadenitripp @laf131 To our knowledge we're the only major image model that doesn't totally rewrite your prompts with a LLM before going into the system! Do you have a preference for how our system should behave if you put something that's too long into it? We find that most of the long prompts have details that have no impact on the image so it might be easier for most people to do our best to losslessly compress the prompt then let them know it happened etc.

items.title: AI data center capex is no longer neutral: GPU capacity now defines who can train and deploy models ...

items.title: @emollick The problem is that everything you can get by testing something new, your competitor can c...
items.content:
@emollick The problem is that everything you can get by testing something new, your competitor can catch up in a matter of a couple of month. See how virtually at the same moment both Claude and ChatGPT have become the next level agentic coders.

There's still no moat and unlikely will be because this is how ML work: you label some data and you get what you need. The question is just what data to label.

There's still no moat and unlikely will be because this is how ML work: you label some data and you get what you need. The question is just what data to label.

items.title: @gsmbk I don’t know, but my instinct is most people want to hack in user space not kernel space, so ...
items.content: @gsmbk I don’t know, but my instinct is most people want to hack in user space not kernel space, so building on top of a stable set of device drivers already optimized for hardware (eg, Linux) and focusing on a hackable “shell” (in the broadest sense of the word) is probably a ton easier than truly making a hackable kernel. But what’s fun with Codex is… who knows? Maybe all these things we previously thought were so hard you shouldn’t touch them, like OS kernels, are less hard? I can’t wait for the AI native Linus Torvalds to make me question all of my assumptions
items.author: @btaylor
items.summary: Bret Taylor speculates about AI-native OS development, suggesting coding agents might make previously hard problems like OS kernels more accessible.

But what’s fun with Codex is… who knows? Maybe all these things we previously thought were so hard you shouldn’t touch them, like OS kernels, are less hard? You can now attach any Storage Bucket, model or dataset from the Hub as a loc...
items.content:
Introducing hf-mount 

You can now attach any Storage Bucket, model or dataset from the Hub as a local filesystem. It allows you to attach remote storage that is 100x bigger than your local machine's disk. This is also perfect for Agentic storage! Read-write for Storage Buckets, read-only for models and datasets.

Get started by installing hf-mount from the GitHub repo: https://t.co/ahWZAy3Gbu

items.title:
Almost 20,000 people have signed up for my free AI Agent Workshop tomorrow.

I did NOT expect this.
...
items.content:
Almost 20,000 people have signed up for my free AI Agent Workshop tomorrow.

I did NOT expect this.

That number tells me something important though. There are a LOT of people out there who keep hearing about AI agents but haven't had a clear, jargon-free place to actually learn how they work.

That's exactly why I built this.

No coding required. No prior experience required. Just show up curious.

I've spent years translating AI from engineering speak to business professional speak - at IBM, at Amazon, and now for millions - and this is the session where I (hope to) bring it to the world of AI agents.

Tomorrow. March 25. 12pm ET. Free.

https://t.co/XbpoNzFjiB 

If you've been waiting for the right moment to start - this is it.

items.summary: Jason Lopatecki warns that agent failures are scariest when silent - bad output becomes next agent's input and drifts downstream. Runtime visibility is essential.

items.title:
@din0s_ Not actually but kind of (we should update docs)
 
the logic behind this is basically we wan...
items.content:
@din0s_ Not actually but kind of (we should update docs)
 
the logic behind this is basically we want async subagents to run in their own process

In order to do that we need a protocol to communicate with that process

We use agent protocol, of which our deployment platform implements (and we use a bunch internally) which is why it’s probably phrased in that way

But it should work with any deployment option that uses that protocol

I’ll update docs

Does that make sense/seem more reasonable?
items.author: @hwchase17
items.summary: Harrison Chase explains async subagents run in their own process, requiring a protocol for inter-process communication, using agent protocol
items.reasoning: Technical detail about agent architecture design decisions from LangChain CEO
items.summary_html: Harrison Chase explains async subagents run in their own process, requiring a protocol for inter-process communication, using agent protocol
items.content_html:
@din0s_ Not actually but kind of (we should update docs)

the logic behind this is basically we want async subagents to run in their own process

In order to do that we need a protocol to communicate with that process

We use agent protocol, of which our deployment platform implements (and we use a bunch internally) which is why it’s probably phrased in that way

But it should work with any deployment option that uses that protocol

I’ll update docs

Does that make sense/seem more reasonable?

items.title: Every time I visit San Francisco I have the opposite of the “future shock”. I am absolutely floored ...
items.content: Every time I visit San Francisco I have the opposite of the “future shock”. I am absolutely floored by how backward and dilapidated most of the city is, especially the city that prides itself on being on the forefront of the future. There have been barely any significant infrastructure improvements over the past 60 years. When it comes to public transportation alone, you are far better off in any moderately sized Eastern European city for instance.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz criticizes San Francisco's infrastructure, noting it's surprisingly backward for a city at the forefront of tech, with poor public transit compared to Eastern European cities

items.title:
Most “multimodal” AI is just duct tape.

One model for text.
Another for vision.
A pipeline to glue ...
items.content:
Most “multimodal” AI is just duct tape.

One model for text.
Another for vision.
A pipeline to glue it together.

But what happens when text + vision are native in the same model, with a 256K context window?

That is what Moonshot AI just changed.

A thread on what I learned from The Multimodal Model Upgrade…
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon starts a thread on Moonshot AI's Kimi K2.5 multimodal model with native text+vision and 256K context.

items.summary_html: Van Loon starts a thread on Moonshot AI's Kimi K2.5 multimodal model with native text+vision and 256K context.
items.content_html:
Most “multimodal” AI is just duct tape.

One model for text.

Another for vision.

A pipeline to glue it together.

But what happens when text + vision are native in the same model, with a 256K context window?

That is what Moonshot AI just changed.

A thread on what I learned from The Multimodal Model Upgrade…

items.title: A new learning curriculum on @ChapterPal: Prep reading for the AI problem framing and metrics interv...
items.content:
A new learning curriculum on @ChapterPal: Prep reading for the AI problem framing and metrics interview

This curriculum prepares learners for AI problem framing and metrics interviews by covering the fundamental theory and practice of evaluating machine learning models.

The papers establish foundational concepts including evaluation metrics for imbalanced datasets (precision-recall and ROC curves), the theory of proper scoring rules for assessing probabilistic forecasts, and statistical methods for comparing algorithms fairly.

The curriculum then progresses to practical considerations such as cost-sensitive learning, calibration of neural network confidence estimates, and uncertainty quantification.

It addresses real-world challenges including the gap between benchmark performance and actual generalization (shortcut learning and overfitting), detection of model failures and out-of-distribution examples, adversarial robustness, and behavioral testing beyond simple accuracy metrics.

The collection also covers interpreting model predictions through techniques like SHAP, fairness and bias considerations in supervised learning, and the critical systems-level perspective that recognizes evaluation metrics exist within broader machine learning pipelines prone to technical debt.

Together, these papers provide both theoretical rigor and practical wisdom for framing problems, selecting appropriate metrics, and understanding what model performance actually means in context.

Active learning with AI tutor: https://t.co/xsmBkDUywH

While leading the pack among open models, MolmoWeb has limitations. 

It can misread text, lose track after a wrong action, & struggle with vague prompts. For safety reasons, it’s also not trained on tasks with logins/financial transactions. These remain active research areas.

items.title:
AI coding tools are great at helping new developers ramp faster. 

Mike Cannon-Brookes talks about w...
items.content:
AI coding tools are great at helping new developers ramp faster. 

Mike Cannon-Brookes talks about why that’s a good thing, but also why it doesn’t remove the need for experienced engineers. 

Someone still has to look at the code, understand it, and stand behind it. It’s a useful way to think about how teams can scale with these tools.

items.content:
My takeaway:

The real upgrade is not just multimodality.

It is collapsing text, vision, long context, and reasoning into one coherent workflow.

That is how you move from demos to production-grade AI systems.

I break it all down in my new video on Kimi K2.5 by Moonshot AI.

items.title:
I also like the operational thinking behind it.

You get:

→ Thinking mode for deeper reasoning and ...
items.content:
I also like the operational thinking behind it.

You get:

→ Thinking mode for deeper reasoning and multi-step tool calls
→ Faster mode when latency matters
→ JSON mode, streaming, file-based Q&A
→ Token estimation so you can predict cost before heavy vision jobs

And it is OpenAI SDK compatible, so migration friction is low.

This is systems thinking, not just model scaling.

items.content_html:
I also like the operational thinking behind it.

You get:

→ Thinking mode for deeper reasoning and multi-step tool calls

→ Faster mode when latency matters

→ JSON mode, streaming, file-based Q&A

→ Token estimation so you can predict cost before heavy vision jobs

And it is OpenAI SDK compatible, so migration friction is low.

This is systems thinking, not just model scaling.

items.title:
The biggest shift is architectural.

This is not text routed to a vision model.

It is a native mult...
items.content:
The biggest shift is architectural.

This is not text routed to a vision model.

It is a native multimodal model.

→ Send base64 images or even videos directly into a chat completion request
→ Reason across text and visuals in the same workflow
→ No fragile orchestration layer

For builders, that means fewer moving parts and fewer failure points.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon highlights Kimi K2.5's native multimodal architecture vs. typical pipelined approaches.
items.reasoning: Technical insight into native multimodal architecture advantages.

It is a native multimodal model.

→ Send base64 images or even videos directly into a chat completion request

→ Reason across text and visuals in the same workflow

→ No fragile orchestration layer

For builders, that means fewer moving parts and fewer failure points.

items.title:
Here is how you can deploy a claw agent without spending 10 hours in configuration hell.

This is wh...
items.content:
Here is how you can deploy a claw agent without spending 10 hours in configuration hell.

This is what I recommend to anyone who wants a taste of autonomous agents doing things for them, but doesn't have time to deal with a complex setup.

Here, I'm partnering with Blink. You can spin a new agent with their platform faster than anywhere else.

It takes 4 steps:

1. Describe the job you want your agent to do
2. Deploy the agent
3. Set up the tools you want your agent to access
4. Set up the channels you want to communicate with the agent

That's it.

items.title:
🎲 Clearbit just nuked their free logo service

You could call it by for example:

> logo.clearbit .c...
items.content:
🎲 Clearbit just nuked their free logo service

You could call it by for example:

> logo.clearbit .com/microsoft .com

To get Microsoft's logo

What's sad is they didn't just 301 redirect it to another service, like Google, which means lots of sites that rely on it to show logos of companies (like many of my sites) now break

They got acquired by Hubspot, so a cool fix for @dharmesh that I DM'd already would be to 301 it to Google's logo API:

> https://s2.googleusercontent .com/s2/favicons?domain=microsoft .com&sz=128

Or DuckDuckGo's logo API:

> https://icons.duckduckgo .com/ip3/microsoft.com.ico

I love websites that offer a service and then when they abandon it, respect the web and somehow keep it going or 301 redirect it to keep the web functioning, because when you browse older sites on the web, everything is just broken because nobody ever maintains shit after they sell the company!

items.title: The cluster**ck that are our airports become ever so often is one of the main reason that I got a sm...
items.content: The cluster**ck that are our airports become ever so often is one of the main reason that I got a small RV a few years ago. Unfortunately long road trips can be extremely exhausting, so I haven’t been doing them as much as I used to. But hopefully at some point over the next few years fully unsupervised level 5 autonomy becomes a widespread reality, in which case I might ditch flying for good for domestic travel.

items.title: Stop scrolling! Look... here is some exciting news from @NewRelic — they are advancing Observability...
items.content:
Stop scrolling! Look... here is some exciting news from @NewRelic — they are advancing Observability into the Agentic Era with their announcement of the New Relic Agentic Platform.

💥Observability Beyond Human Scale!🏆

This is not a new "feature", but an AI-powered platform with an intelligence layer that connects telemetry, system relationships, and operational context in order to maximize the performance, response time, and automation of observability functions across your enterprise systems.

First, see and learn about this in the on-demand keynote address that was presented at the #NewRelicAdvance 2026 live virtual event on Feb.24. Start here:
https://t.co/vBzmxHkSzr

For a full roundup of New Relic Advance 2026, look here:
https://t.co/ieGp6rlIzs

Second, read more about the new observability platform in their blog "Introducing New Relic Agentic Platform" at:
https://t.co/ZyjU6wzmp3

Why? The New Relic Agentic Platform is designed to help teams in these ways:
🟡Observe complex, dynamic behavior as it unfolds
🟡Maintain situational awareness across distributed systems
🟡Reduce manual correlation and swivel-chair troubleshooting
🟡Support both human operators and agent-driven workflows
🟡Shorten the distance between signal and informed action

You want your Observability Platform to answer these questions in real-time: What happened? Why did it happen? What is the correct response? @NewRelic delivers all that with its AI-powered Agentic Platform for Observability.

The @NewRelic Agentic Platform moves observability from monitoring and static reporting into continuous insights and contextual understanding for timely action. Don't wait! Act now to see how this is the must-have observability platform for your organization in the Agentic Era.

That is not a spec sheet flex.

That is:

→ Entire codebases...
items.content:
Then there is the 256K context window.

That is not a spec sheet flex.

That is:

→ Entire codebases in context
→ Long legal or technical documents without chunking gymnastics
→ Multi-turn agent workflows that do not lose the plot

If you are building internal copilots or agent systems, context limits are usually the bottleneck.

This removes a big one.

items.title: The world's largest annual cybersecurity conference #RSAC2026 is into Day 2. @Commvault unveils more...
items.content:
The world's largest annual cybersecurity conference #RSAC2026 is into Day 2. @Commvault unveils more of their innovation and strength in delivering enterprise #CyberResilience and #IdentityResilience, amplified by the #PowerOfCommunity at @OneRSAC.

Following on yesterday's partnership announcements (with Microsoft Security and TIME), and other recent integrations (with CloudSEK and Okta), today #Commvault announces a strategic alliance with NetApp to deliver an integrated solution for enterprise data protection, cyber resilience, and rapid recovery across on-prem and cloud environments—delivering confidence to organizations that their data are always available, immutable, and recoverable:
https://t.co/I0DOANsyKl

#RSAC #ResOps #ResOpsRumble #Sponsored
items.author: @KirkDBorne

items.summary_html: Kirk Borne covers Commvault's strategic alliance with NetApp for enterprise data protection and cyber resilience, announced at RSAC 2026.
items.content_html:
The world's largest annual cybersecurity conference #RSAC2026 is into Day 2. @Commvault unveils more of their innovation and strength in delivering enterprise #CyberResilience and #IdentityResilience, amplified by the #PowerOfCommunity at @OneRSAC.

Following on yesterday's partnership announcements (with Microsoft Security and TIME), and other recent integrations (with CloudSEK and Okta), today #Commvault announces a strategic alliance with NetApp to deliver an integrated solution for enterprise data protection, cyber resilience, and rapid recovery across on-prem and cloud environments—delivering confidence to organizations that their data are always available, immutable, and recoverable:

https://t.co/I0DOANsyKl

#RSAC #ResOps #ResOpsRumble #Sponsored

items.title:
The bigger opportunity is APIs.

We saw 4G enable Uber, Grubhub, the app economy.

Now 5G + network ...
items.content:
The bigger opportunity is APIs.

We saw 4G enable Uber, Grubhub, the app economy.

Now 5G + network APIs enable developers to build applications that assume guaranteed performance.

That is a fundamental shift.

When performance becomes programmable, telcos move up the value chain.

From connectivity providers → to experience platforms.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon argues 5G + network APIs represent a fundamental shift enabling telcos to become experience platforms.
items.reasoning: Infrastructure perspective relevant to edge AI and connected services.

Premium connectivity will redefine ARPU, but only if product and marketing teams stop ...
items.content:
My takeaway:

Premium connectivity will redefine ARPU, but only if product and marketing teams stop selling speed and start selling experiences.

The demand is already there.

If 60% of customers will pay for performance at an event, what other moments are we under-monetizing?

Watch the full conversation to see how this plays out in 2026, and what it means for your strategy.

items.title:
5G network slicing is the unlock.

It allows operators to create purpose-built slices of the network...
items.content:
5G network slicing is the unlock.

It allows operators to create purpose-built slices of the network for specific use cases:

→ Ultra-low latency for gaming
→ High uplink quality for live streaming
→ Secure, fraud-protected transactions for retail
→ Immersive AR and VR experiences

This is not theoretical.

Operators in Asia are already monetizing event-based passes, four-day upgrades, even influencer packages.

items.title: What are the Core Value Offerings from @Commvault at #RSAC2026? ...They are #IdentityResilience, #Cy...
items.content:
What are the Core Value Offerings from @Commvault at #RSAC2026? ...They are #IdentityResilience, #CyberResilience, #PowerOfCommunity, and #ResOps ...

• ResOps — Unified Resilience Operations: When resilience and operations work as one, organizations can recover fast and stay operational during attacks.

• Identity Resilience — Automated, policy-driven protection and granular point-in-time recovery (for Active Directory, Entra ID, Okta) from misconfigs, disruptions, and identity-driven cyberattacks.

• Threat Scan & Verified Clean Recovery — Defense-in-depth threat hunting integrated with AI-enabled Synthetic Recovery delivers confidence that what gets restored is clean.

• Data & AI Security with Satori — DSPM and real-time access governance across structured, unstructured, and vector databases — unified into resilience workflows.

• Bi-Directional Security Integration with CrowdStrike: — Shared detection and recovery context across CrowdStrike Falcon Next-Gen SIEM and Commvault Cloud.

items.title:
We are watching a shift from selling bandwidth → to selling outcomes.

Speed is table stakes.

Perfo...
items.content:
We are watching a shift from selling bandwidth → to selling outcomes.

Speed is table stakes.

Performance is premium.

Think about a World Cup stadium:

→ Ordering food from your seat
→ Streaming live as an influencer
→ Placing secure mobile payments
→ Playing real-time AR experiences

In those moments, latency and reliability matter more than raw Mbps.

That is where differentiated connectivity comes in.

items.content:
Starting soon at #RSAC2026 >> @Commvault #RSAC speaking session on "When Attackers Swing at You: Don't Tap Out. Instead, Recover Fast with ResOps"

🟣Date/Time:  Tuesday, March 25 at 2:25pm PST
🟣@OneRSAC Location:  Moscone South Esplanade
🟣#Commvault Speakers:  Bill O'Connell (Chief Security Officer) & Chris Bevil (Global Cyber Resilience & AI)

✔️Summary: Security teams face constant threats but often lack a clear view of their resilience. This session explores how Resilience Operations #ResOps helps organizations focused on preparedness and recovery. Using identity systems as an example, we'll cover how to assess dependencies, test recovery plans, and build measurable readiness to stay operational during attacks.

⚛️What is ResOps? Learn here: https://t.co/ZPtXOI7u2L

⚛️And don't miss the ResOps Rumble. Find out more (what, where, when) on the RSAC 2026 Event Page: https://t.co/0xjG5w6dyN
(Hint: Booth S-0634)

⚛️Book a Meeting with Commvault: https://t.co/o0RRfDXF1H
(Yes! at Booth S-0634)

items.title:
Nice hot take but structurally false I think

The European immigration debate is about mass, permane...
items.content:
Nice hot take but structurally false I think

The European immigration debate is about mass, permanent, involuntary immigration: people fleeing poverty, settling permanently in Europe, heavily using welfare systems and changing demographics irreversibly 

Digital nomads are voluntary, usually temporary, high-income and pay their way. They're more like rich tourists who stay longer than any regular immigration archetype. Digital nomads don't claim welfare, they don't affect public housing queues, and because they're not resident, they need to keep having income remotely to pay for most things privately (like healthcare)

There's no data attributing any significant crime to digital nomads in South East Asia. Actual crime there comes from different sources: Chinese-origin criminal networks (see the kidnappings in Thailand brought to Myanmar and Cambodia to scam people. 

Westerners on laptops drinking their iced latte are not showing up in any crime data in particular. Maybe some Russians in Bali for sure but that's not most digital nomads, and that's just because they're Russian!

The average digital nomad earns $85,000/year and spends about 35% of that into the local economy: on housing, coworking, cafes, food, transport, directly supporting local jobs and small businesses

Many countries around the world are trying to attract MORE digital nomads not LESS, like Thailand, Indonesia and Malaysia rolling out special digital nomad visa for them, governments generally like digital nomads. They're a good contribution to the countries bringing lots of money, high tech (and high  IQ) tech workers which can rub off on the locals

The only argument you can make is gentrification, more digital nomads and tourists in a place can gentrify a place. See what happened in a place like Canggu in Bali. But that's more about too many hipster cafes, coworkings and brunch places and overbuilding of houses, which can be avoided by better regulation and zoning. Thailand for example has barely any of these issues. Luckily Bali is big so most of it is unaffected

Anyway, apart from that digital nomads are generally a net positive contribution, while the current mass immigration in Europe is a net negative. And that's now provable with data. 

I'm an immigrant myself, and digital nomad, and we should change the narrative not that immigration is bad, but that there's good and bad immigration

Europe is getting mostly bad immigration now which is causing severe social and economic issues now

So yes it is completely logical and not hypocritical to see DNs go to South East Asia while they complain about immigrants in Europe, you just have to look at the data to get it

items.title:
So many stories like this in Bali

You book a beautiful luxury couple massage 

Beautiful temple wit...
items.content:
So many stories like this in Bali

You book a beautiful luxury couple massage 

Beautiful temple with a jungle garden and nice staff welcoming you with a welcome drink

Then you lie down and 15 minutes into the massage the trash burning starts, the entire room gets cooked and fills with smoggy air

You and your clothes all smell after

Staff says "normaaallll in Baliiii"

It's really a paradise lost

items.title:
@HanSoLMAO @JayVander_ My opinion about these things has been pretty consistent

I wasn't doing blac...
items.content:
@HanSoLMAO @JayVander_ My opinion about these things has been pretty consistent

I wasn't doing black BLM or rainbow logo or woke stuff on https://t.co/UXK5AFqCaQ 10 years ago either when everyone else was

I didn't like either the radical left or extreme right

My foundation has been pretty simple: anyone can be whoever they wanna be and do whatever they wanna do if it doesn't reduce other people's freedoms

Being a structural net negative socially and economically to your host country is ABSOLUTELY reducing other people's freedoms, esp of locals, and digital nomads don't do that

There's nothing racist about saying there's good immigrants and bad immigrants. Both can be any skin color

I'm happy to argue against arguments but this is just ad hominem I think because you ran out of arguments as did OP btw
